name: next-static
description: Next.js App Router + TypeScript + Tailwind for landing pages, marketing sites, and frontend-first sites.

commands.install: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.dev: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.lint: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.build: Defined by the approved scaffold task.

ci_policy: Core CI remains stack-neutral. Add Next.js lint/build/security checks only after a Next.js project is scaffolded by an approved task.
deploy_notes: Deploy as a static or frontend-first site after stack approval, scaffold verification, product/design approval, and final delivery gates.
services: Static hosting or managed frontend hosting
best_for: Frontend-first sites with strong SEO needs
pros: Strong React ecosystem and SEO support.
Good path from static pages to richer frontend behavior.
Large hiring and documentation ecosystem.
cons: More moving parts than Astro for mostly static content.
Dependency tree and build tooling need ongoing audit attention.
Backend features require later service/runtime decisions.
scaffold_policy: No Next.js app is bundled. Scaffold Next.js only after the user approves this profile and an approved scaffold task records the target directory and commands.
selection_questions: Is the site mostly marketing content with a few interactive sections?
Does the team prefer React and TypeScript?
Will content updates come from code, a CMS, or a manager workflow?
Should deployment favor managed hosting simplicity or VPS/VDS control?

deployment_options.name: VPS/VDS
deployment_options.best_for: Teams that need server control, custom runtime processes, reverse proxy rules, or bundled backend services.
pros: Maximum control over runtime, logs, backups, and server configuration.
Can host frontend, backend, database helpers, queues, or custom services together.
cons: Requires server administration, patching, monitoring, backups, and incident response.
Higher operational risk for non-technical owners.

deployment_options.name: Managed hosting
deployment_options.best_for: Marketing and frontend-first sites where simple deploys, CDN, previews, and low maintenance matter most.
pros: Fast setup, previews, CDN, HTTPS, and rollback are usually built in.
Less server maintenance for the client.
cons: Less low-level control than VPS/VDS.
Provider limits, pricing, and supported runtimes can constrain custom backends.

name: next-fullstack
description: Next.js App Router + TypeScript + Tailwind, with backend and data decisions made later, for SaaS or app-like sites.

commands.install: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.dev: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.lint: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.build: Defined by the approved scaffold task.

ci_policy: Core CI remains stack-neutral. Add frontend, backend, database, and integration checks only after concrete service decisions are recorded.
deploy_notes: Choose hosting, database, auth, storage, payment/request flows, environment handling, and backup policy in approved tasks before release.
languages: SQL when a database is approved
services: Payment or request workflow when approved
best_for: Sites with auth, dashboards, or forms backed by data
Projects that may grow from marketing into product workflows
pros: One TypeScript stack can cover UI and server behavior.
Good fit for auth, dashboards, previews, forms, and incremental product features.
Many deployment providers support the pattern directly.
cons: More architecture decisions are required before implementation.
Operational complexity grows with database, auth, payments, and background work.
Provider choice can strongly affect cost and maintenance.
scaffold_policy: No fullstack app is bundled. Scaffold Next.js and backend/service wiring only after product, design, data, auth, and deployment decisions are approved in tracked tasks.
selection_questions: Does the site need auth, dashboards, admin tools, or persistent user data?
Which data objects and workflows must exist at launch?
Who will operate database backups, secrets, migrations, and monitoring?
Should deployment favor managed fullstack hosting or VPS/VDS control?

deployment_options.name: VPS/VDS
deployment_options.best_for: Fullstack projects needing custom services, strict server control, private networking, or one-server deployment.
pros: Control over Node runtime, reverse proxy, logs, cron, backups, and service placement.
Can combine app, backend workers, and supporting services when approved.
cons: Requires DevOps ownership for security patches, uptime, firewall, backups, and monitoring.
Scaling and rollback need explicit setup.

deployment_options.name: Managed hosting
deployment_options.best_for: Fullstack apps that fit provider runtimes and need easier previews, autoscaling, and managed deploys.
pros: Lower operations burden and faster delivery for common Next.js patterns.
Preview deployments, HTTPS, logs, and rollback are usually easier.
cons: Backend/runtime limits may affect long-running jobs, websockets, or custom infrastructure.
Costs and vendor lock-in can grow with traffic and services.

name: astro-content
description: Astro for SEO/content-heavy sites, blogs, and documentation.

commands.install: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.dev: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.lint: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.build: Defined by the approved scaffold task.

ci_policy: Core CI remains stack-neutral. Add Astro checks after the Astro project is scaffolded by an approved task.
deploy_notes: Deploy as static content unless an approved task adds server behavior, CMS integration, or form handling.
pros: Excellent performance for mostly static content.
Simple deployment and smaller client-side JavaScript by default.
Content collections fit blogs, docs, and structured pages well.
cons: Less natural for complex app-like interaction than React-first stacks.
Backend, auth, and dashboards require extra services or a different profile.
Some teams may have less Astro experience than React/Next.js.
scaffold_policy: No Astro app is bundled. Scaffold Astro only after the user approves this profile and content/source decisions are recorded.
selection_questions: Is most of the site public content, articles, services, or documentation?
How often will content change, and who edits it?
Does the launch need complex logged-in user flows?
Should deployment be simple managed/static hosting or a VPS/VDS with custom server needs?

deployment_options.name: VPS/VDS
deployment_options.best_for: Static sites that need custom server rules, colocated services, or a client-owned server environment.
pros: Full control over web server, redirects, cache headers, and backups.
Can host static output beside approved backend services.
cons: More maintenance than static managed hosting.
Manual deploy, TLS, monitoring, and rollback need setup.

deployment_options.name: Managed hosting
deployment_options.best_for: Static/content sites that need fast CDN delivery, simple deploys, previews, and low maintenance.
pros: Usually the simplest and fastest option for Astro content sites.
CDN, HTTPS, previews, and rollback are often included.
cons: Less server-level customization.
Dynamic backend features require external services or another deployment pattern.

name: sveltekit

commands.install: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.dev: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.lint: Defined by the approved scaffold task.
commands.build: Defined by the approved scaffold task.

ci_policy: Core CI remains stack-neutral. Add SvelteKit checks after the SvelteKit project is scaffolded and adapter/runtime decisions are recorded.
deploy_notes: Choose SvelteKit adapter, server/static mode, database/auth needs, and runtime target after product/design approval.
best_for: Teams that prefer Svelte's component model
pros: Concise components and strong interactive UI ergonomics.
Can target static or server runtimes through adapters.
Often less boilerplate than React-heavy alternatives.
cons: Smaller hiring and ecosystem footprint than React/Next.js.
Adapter/runtime decisions matter for deployment and backend behavior.
Some integrations may have less mature examples than React equivalents.
scaffold_policy: No SvelteKit app is bundled. Scaffold SvelteKit only after the user approves this profile and adapter/runtime decisions are recorded.
selection_questions: Is the project primarily an interactive app rather than content-heavy pages?
Does the team already know or prefer Svelte?
Should the adapter target static hosting, managed server hosting, or VPS/VDS?
What backend, auth, or data flows are required at launch?

deployment_options.name: VPS/VDS
deployment_options.best_for: SvelteKit server deployments needing custom Node runtime, server hooks, colocated services, or deeper ops control.
pros: Strong runtime control and flexible adapter/server configuration.
Can run custom services alongside the app when approved.
cons: Requires explicit server operations, security updates, monitoring, and rollback setup.
More setup work than managed adapter hosting.

deployment_options.name: Managed hosting
deployment_options.best_for: SvelteKit projects that fit a supported adapter and benefit from previews, CDN, and low-maintenance deploys.
pros: Simpler deploy and maintenance when the adapter matches project needs.
Preview, HTTPS, logs, and rollback are usually easier.
cons: Provider adapter limits can constrain runtime behavior.
Long-running or custom server workloads may need a different target.

name: custom
description: A user-defined stack selected after clarification.

commands.install: Record the selected stack install command in STACK.md.
commands.dev: Record the selected stack dev command in STACK.md.
commands.lint: Record the selected stack lint command in STACK.md.
commands.build: Record the selected stack build command in STACK.md.

ci_policy: Core CI remains stack-neutral until custom stack checks are explicitly documented.
deploy_notes: Record deploy notes in STACK.md or an approved task before release.
best_for: Teams that already know the required language/framework
pros: Can match the client's existing team, hosting, or integration constraints.
Avoids forcing a default stack that does not fit the project.
cons: Requires clearer documentation of commands, checks, hosting, and support ownership.
May need more setup before agents can safely scaffold or verify work.
scaffold_policy: No custom stack is bundled. Record the user's approved language, framework, services, commands, and hosting target before scaffolding.
selection_questions: Which language, framework, database, and hosting constraints are already fixed?
Who will maintain the stack after launch?
Which commands prove install, dev, lint, test, build, and deploy readiness?
Should publication use VPS/VDS, managed hosting, or a provider-specific target?

deployment_options.name: VPS/VDS
deployment_options.best_for: Custom stacks needing root/server control, uncommon runtimes, private services, or client-owned infrastructure.
pros: Most flexible for uncommon runtimes and custom service layouts.
The client controls server configuration, logs, backups, and network rules.
cons: Requires explicit operations owner and maintenance budget.
Security, updates, backups, and monitoring are the client's responsibility.

deployment_options.name: Managed hosting
deployment_options.best_for: Custom stacks that fit a managed provider and benefit from lower maintenance.
pros: Lower operational load when the provider supports the chosen stack.
Managed deploy, HTTPS, logs, scaling, and rollback may be available.
cons: Provider support may not match every custom runtime.
Limits, pricing, and migration paths must be reviewed before approval.
